content:
First, note that for any \(d\)-digit positive integer \(n\), the product of its digits is at most \(9^d\), whereas the right‐hand side
\[
n^2 - 15n - 27
\]
grows like \(n^2\).  In fact one checks that as soon as \(n\ge100\) (so \(d\ge3\)), 
\[
n^2 - 15n - 27 \;>\; 10^{2\cdot3-2}=10^4 \;>\;9^3=729\;\ge\;\text{(product of digits)},
\]
so no solutions occur for \(n\ge100\).  Hence we need only test \(1\le n\le99\).

1)  If \(1\le n\le9\), the product of digits is \(n\), and the equation becomes
\[
n \;=\; n^2-15n-27
\;\Longrightarrow\;
n^2-16n-27=0,
\]
which has no integer solutions in \(1\le n\le9\).

2)  If \(10\le n=10a+b\le99\) with \(1\le a\le9,\;0\le b\le9\), the product of digits is \(ab\), so we solve
\[
ab \;=\;(10a+b)^2-15(10a+b)-27.
\]
Rewriting,
\[
0 \;=\; (10a+b)^2-15(10a+b)-27 \;-\;ab
\;=\;100a^2+19ab+b^2-150a-15b-27.
\]
For each \(a=1,2,\dots,9\) one checks the quadratic in \(b\)
\[
b^2+(19a-15)b+(100a^2-150a-27)=0
\]
for integer roots \(0\le b\le9\).  The only choice that works is
\[
a=1,\quad b=7,
\]
giving \(n=17\).  Indeed
\[
\text{(product of digits)}=1\cdot7=7,
\quad
17^2-15\cdot17-27=289-255-27=7.
\]

No other \(n\) in \([1,99]\) satisfies the equation, and none occur for \(n\ge100\).  Hence the only solution is \(n=17\), and so the requested sum is

\[
\boxed{17}.
\]
